summary:
The Dacian Wars (101–102 AD and 105–106 AD) were the two major military campaigns by Emperor Trajan against the Dacian Kingdom (centered in modern Romania) under King Decebalus — campaigns that resulted in the conquest of Dacia, its transformation into a Roman province, and the seizure of enormous quantities of Dacian gold and silver that helped fund Trajan's extensive building programs in Rome. Dacia had been a sophisticated Iron Age state with impressive hilltop fortresses, skilled metallurgy, and a writing system; Decebalus had already extracted a humiliating treaty from Emperor Domitian.

Trajan's first campaign (101–102 AD) ended with Decebalus accepting Roman terms but retaining his throne. When Decebalus violated the treaty and resumed anti-Roman activities, Trajan launched a second campaign (105–106 AD), systematically destroying Dacian fortresses, capturing the capital Sarmizegetusa, and driving Decebalus to suicide. The conquest was commemorated on [[event:2021|Trajan's Column]] in Rome, whose spiraling relief carvings provide an extraordinarily detailed visual record of the Roman army, its equipment, tactics, and the Dacian enemy. Dacia became the only major Roman province established north of the Danube — the ancestor of modern Romania, which preserves a Romance language (Romanian) derived from the Latin of Roman colonists despite being separated from the rest of the Roman-speaking world by centuries of migration. The Roman colonists and legionaries who settled Dacia are among the Romans' most distant and enduring cultural legacy.
